skill.name: api-integration-specialist
skill.description: Expert in integrating third-party APIs with proper authentication, error handling, rate limiting, and retry logic. Use when integrating REST APIs, GraphQL endpoints, webhooks, or external services. Specializes in OAuth flows, API key management, request/response transformation, and building robust API clients.
skill.summary: Guides secure, reliable REST, GraphQL, webhook, and provider API integrations.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto findings were reviewed and are false positives from Markdown code fences, JavaScript template literals, and non-cryptographic example text. Confirmed residual risk is medium because the skill intentionally teaches network integrations and environment-based credentials, which require careful user handling.

medium_findings.title: Network Integration Guidance Requires User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes fetch calls, API client request methods, and provider SDK examples. This is legitimate for the stated purpose, but generated integrations can send data to external services if users configure unsafe endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct network-call examples are present and match the skill purpose. I did not find evidence of unauthorized exfiltration or hardcoded malicious endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Examples Use Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: The skill references API keys, OAuth client secrets, webhook secrets, and provider tokens through environment variables. This is a recommended pattern, but it still handles sensitive credentials and should be treated as elevated risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly access environment variables that commonly hold secrets. The context is legitimate credential configuration, not secret theft.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across many lines. Review shows these are Markdown code fences or JavaScript template literals inside documentation, not executable shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown skill document and the flagged backticks are visible documentation syntax. No executable script file or shell invocation was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm locations, but the reviewed lines describe the skill, rate limit timing values, or unrelated variables. The webhook example uses HMAC SHA-256 and timing-safe comparison.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited weak-crypto lines do not contain MD5, SHA-1, or deprecated algorithms. The nearby real cryptographic example uses SHA-256 for webhook signatures.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: No evidence found of override instructions, claims of pre-approval, system-message impersonation, or instructions to skip security review in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search and contextual review did not find suspicious prompt-injection phrases. The finding is limited to the reviewed SKILL.md file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External HTTP Request Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation teaches dynamic API requests with fetch and reusable request methods. Users should validate base URLs, endpoints, timeouts, and allowed destinations before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network request patterns are explicit and central to the skill. They are not malicious by themselves, but they can move data externally.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Configuration Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation references API keys, webhook secrets, OAuth secrets, and provider tokens. Users should avoid exposing these values in prompts, logs, generated files, or client-side code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential examples are explicit and semantically tied to provider authentication. They use acceptable storage patterns but still involve sensitive data.

content.value_statement: API integrations often fail because authentication, retries, and rate limits are handled inconsistently. This skill provides practical patterns for secure clients, webhooks, and provider SDKs.
actual_capabilities: Designs REST API client wrappers with shared request and response handling.
Explains OAuth, API key, JWT, and webhook signature patterns.
Provides retry, timeout, rate limit, and pagination guidance.
Shows provider examples for Stripe, SendGrid, and Twilio integrations.
Helps transform external API responses into application models.
limitations: Does not include executable helper scripts or automated API testing tools.
Requires users to verify provider-specific SDK versions and API changes.
Example code must be adapted before production use.
Does not replace security review for payment, messaging, or identity systems.

use_cases.title: Build a Payment Integration
use_cases.description: Create a secure payment API flow with environment-based secrets, retries, and structured error handling.

use_cases.title: Standardize API Client Patterns
use_cases.description: Define common request handling, pagination, rate limits, and response transformation for a service team.

use_cases.title: Verify Webhook Processing
use_cases.description: Review webhook signature verification and failure handling before connecting external event sources.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a REST API integration for [service]. Include authentication, required endpoints, error handling, and test cases.

prompt_templates.title: Design an API Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an API client wrapper for [service]. Include request structure, retries, timeouts, pagination, and response transformation.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Webhook Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my webhook integration for [provider]. Focus on signature verification, replay protection, parsing, logging, and failure responses.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Production Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this production API integration plan. Identify reliability, security, rate limit, monitoring, and rollback risks.

output_examples.input: Plan a SendGrid email integration for a SaaS onboarding flow.
output: Authentication should use a secret manager or environment variable.
The client should centralize sender identity, template selection, retries, and provider errors.
Tests should cover invalid recipients, rate limits, provider outages, and duplicate sends.

output_examples.input: Review a Stripe webhook design before launch.
output: Verify the provider signature before parsing business fields.
Return clear responses for invalid signatures, duplicate events, and unsupported event types.
Store processed event identifiers to prevent replay and duplicate fulfillment.

output_examples.input: Create guidance for a paginated customer import API.
output: Use cursor pagination when the provider supports it.
Track progress after each page so failed imports can resume.
Add rate limit handling and monitoring for long-running imports.

best_practices: Keep credentials in managed secrets or environment variables, never in committed source files.
Add timeout, retry, rate limit, and circuit breaker behavior before production use.
Validate webhook signatures and record processed event identifiers.
anti_patterns: Hardcoding provider credentials in source code or prompt text.
Retrying every error without checking status codes or idempotency.
Trusting webhook payloads before verifying provider signatures.

faq.question: Does this skill execute API calls by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation guidance with example patterns. Users must implement and run integrations in their own projects.

faq.question: Which API styles does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers REST clients, GraphQL-style planning, OAuth flows, API keys, webhooks, pagination, and provider SDK examples.

faq.question: Can it help with OAuth?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains authorization code flow concepts and where client identifiers, secrets, redirects, and scopes fit.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can guide production design, but each integration still needs provider documentation, testing, and security review.

faq.question: Does it store or collect secrets?
faq.answer: No evidence found that the skill stores or collects secrets. It references environment variables as example configuration.

faq.question: Why does the audit show risk factors?
faq.answer: The skill discusses network calls and credentials. These are legitimate for API integrations but require careful handling.
